planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile loam in full sun; Bruce is partially self-fertile but bears more heavily when cross-pollinated by Methley or another low-chill Japanese plum variety; requires 250–400 chill hours.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile loam in full sun; Bruce is a classic Gulf Coast Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) developed by B.W. Stone at the Texas A&M Experiment Station in the early twentieth century and released in 1952 — one of the first specifically developed low-chill plum varieties adapted to the warm winters of the Gulf South. Bruce requires only 250–400 chill hours and ripens in late May to early June in zone 9, producing heavy crops of medium to large, red-skinned, yellow-fleshed plums with sweet, juicy, pleasant flavor that makes it excellent for fresh eating, jams, and preserves. The tree is vigorous, productive, and fairly disease-resistant under Gulf South conditions — it handles the humid climate better than many other plum varieties. Bruce is partially self-fertile but benefits significantly from cross-pollination: planting with Methley or another low-chill Japanese plum reliably doubles production. Prune annually in late winter to maintain open-center structure at 10–12 feet; thinning fruit to 3–4 inches after June drop produces full-sized, high-quality plums. Brown rot is the primary disease challenge for plums in humid climates; preventive fungicide applications during bloom and as fruit develops reduce losses significantly.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or bare-root trees in late winter to early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; Saijo is astringent when firm and must be fully soft before eating; it is self-fertile and requires minimal chill hours (100–200).

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Saijo is an astringent Japanese persimmon of Japanese origin — widely considered one of the finest-flavored of all persimmon cultivars, with elongated, narrow, yellow-orange to orange fruits and exceptional rich, honey-sweet flavor that persimmon enthusiasts describe as superior to any non-astringent variety. Saijo is astringent when firm — meaning the fruit must fully ripen and soften to a jelly-like texture before the astringency disappears and the full sweetness and flavor develop. This is not a defect but a different style of persimmon use: fully ripe Saijo is spooned from the skin like a pudding. Saijo ripens in September to October in zone 9, requiring only 100–200 chill hours, making it one of the lowest-chill persimmon cultivars and extremely well adapted to Gulf Coast climates. The tree is handsome, drought-tolerant once established, and remarkably free of serious pests and diseases — one of the easiest fruit trees for Gulf South gardens. The striking orange fruit and vivid orange fall foliage create exceptional ornamental value in the landscape. Saijo can be dried (hoshigaki) to make the traditional Japanese dried persimmon candy — a process that concentrates the intense natural sweetness into a complex, mellow confection.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile soil in full sun; Kimbrough Satsuma is among the most cold-hardy satsuma selections, surviving temperatures as low as 14–18°F with some canopy damage but root survival — a critical advantage in zone 8a–8b where winter freezes periodically occur.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Kimbrough is a cold-hardy satsuma mandarin selection developed and evaluated by the Louisiana State University AgCenter — selected specifically for superior cold hardiness compared to standard Owari satsuma, making it the recommended satsuma cultivar for zone 8a–8b gardens where occasional hard freezes occur. Satsuma mandarins are the most cold-hardy commonly grown citrus — Kimbrough extends the cold hardiness envelope even further, surviving brief exposure to 14–18°F without permanent damage when grown in well-drained soil (saturated roots dramatically reduce cold tolerance). The fruit is easy-peeling, seedless, and has the classic sweet-tart satsuma mandarin flavor that ripens early (October–November) — delivering fresh citrus fruit months before oranges and before the most demanding winter cold arrives in the Gulf South. In zone 9, Kimbrough satsumas perform reliably with minimal cold protection. Satsumas are fast to bearing — often producing some fruit in the second or third year — and are moderate-sized trees that fit comfortably in residential landscapes. Plant in the warmest, most protected microclimate (south-facing, near a wall or fence) for zone 8 plantings.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5), fertile soil in full sun; kumquats are among the most cold-hardy citrus, tolerating temperatures to about 18–22°F, making them suitable for zone 8b–9 with minor frost protection.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Meiwa is the sweetest and most widely recommended kumquat cultivar for fresh eating — a round-fruited kumquat (distinct from the oval Nagami kumquat) with notably sweeter, thicker, nearly non-acidic edible skin and sweet-tart flesh. The sweet skin makes Meiwa kumquats the superior choice for eating whole — both skin and flesh are consumed together, and the sweetness of the Meiwa skin dramatically reduces the puckering tart bite that makes Nagami less pleasant for casual snacking. The small, round, golden-orange fruits appear in clusters on a compact, attractive, evergreen tree that makes an outstanding ornamental specimen even when not in fruit. Kumquats are among the most cold-hardy true citrus — surviving to 18–22°F — which makes them excellent choices for the entire Gulf South and even zone 8b gardens with minor winter protection. The trees are naturally compact and can be maintained at 6–10 feet for easy harvesting. Meiwa kumquat fruit is excellent fresh, sliced in salads, made into marmalade, and candied. The attractive, shiny, dark-green foliage and white, fragrant flowers make it an excellent landscape specimen year-round. Excellent container plant for patios and small gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or rooted cuttings in spring after frost danger passes in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), fertile soil in full sun; figs tolerate poorer soils than most fruit trees but perform best with good drainage and protection from late spring freezes that can damage the crop.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; LSU Gold is a golden-yellow fig cultivar developed by the Louisiana State University Agricultural Center's renowned fig breeding program — one of several outstanding cultivars released by LSU specifically for Gulf Coast garden conditions. The medium-sized fruits have golden-yellow to amber skin and rich, sweet, amber-colored flesh with excellent flavor and lower acidity than common brown-skinned fig varieties. The golden skin and rich, honey-sweet taste make LSU Gold particularly attractive for fresh eating and fresh presentations where the unusual color adds visual appeal. LSU Gold ripens with a main crop in July through September in zone 9 and often produces a lighter breba (early crop) in June. LSU-developed figs are specifically selected for performance in the hot, humid Gulf South — resistance to fig mosaic virus, quality fruit production in high heat and humidity, and productivity in the long Gulf South growing season. Figs are among the easiest and most productive fruit trees for Gulf Coast gardens — drought-tolerant, naturally pest-resistant, fast to bearing (often fruiting in the second year from cuttings), and requiring minimal care beyond keeping the soil moist during fruit development. Propagate easily from dormant hardwood cuttings taken in late winter.
